behaviors.description:
Observed in this behavior: file created in a subdirectory under the watched root must be detected and processed.
Hypothetical elsewhere: transform correctness, output write failures, retry policies.
MOCK-MISS: Yes, recursive watch failures appear at watcher-to-OS boundary.
REAL-INFRA: Yes, depends on real filesystem watch semantics for nested paths.
MOCK-HIDE: Yes, synthetic events can hide missing recursive coverage.
LLM-DEP: No.
MULTI-STEP: No.
Rejection gate: Rejected Integration because behavior text requires real nested filesystem detection ("inside a subdirectory of the watched root").
behaviors.justification: Real watcher behavior for nested directories is OS/filesystem-dependent; in-process fakes can miss real non-recursive watch failures.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a file in `root/subdir/`, and assert exactly one transformed output file appears without restart.

behaviors.description:
Observed in this behavior: second processing request for same path within configured time window is discarded.
Hypothetical elsewhere: duplicate events from OS, queue backpressure, daemon restart behavior.
MOCK-MISS: No, this is primarily dedup decision logic.
REAL-INFRA: No explicit OS/network/subprocess requirement in this behavior.
MOCK-HIDE: No, fake clock + in-process calls can expose window logic bugs.
LLM-DEP: No.
MULTI-STEP: No.
Rejection gate: Rejected Integration/System because behavior text targets "deduplication logic" for same path/time window, not OS event mechanics.
behaviors.justification: A deterministic dedup component with configurable window can be validated at function/object boundary using controlled time.
behaviors.test_description: With a fake clock, call dedup check twice for identical path inside window (expect reject) and outside window (expect accept).

behaviors.description:
Observed in this behavior: OS may emit multiple filesystem events for one write, daemon must process once.
Hypothetical elsewhere: downstream transform idempotency, output dedup.
MOCK-MISS: Yes, duplication pattern is produced by real watcher/OS interaction.
REAL-INFRA: Yes, explicitly depends on operating system event behavior.
MOCK-HIDE: Yes, mocked single event streams can falsely pass.
LLM-DEP: No.
MULTI-STEP: No.
Rejection gate: Rejected Integration because behavior explicitly references "operating system emits multiple filesystem events."
behaviors.justification: The failure mode exists at real filesystem event delivery; only system-level watch tests reliably reproduce it.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon on real watched dir, perform one file write known to trigger multiple events (e.g., create+append+rename pattern), and assert one processing/output record.

behaviors.description:
Observed in this behavior: watched directory is removed then recreated while daemon runs; daemon must resume watching without restart.
Hypothetical elsewhere: crash recovery after process restart, persistent offsets.
MOCK-MISS: Yes, watcher teardown/rebind bugs appear when real directory handles vanish/reappear.
REAL-INFRA: Yes, requires real OS directory deletion/recreation semantics.
MOCK-HIDE: Yes, mocked watcher callbacks can hide lost subscriptions.
LLM-DEP: No.
MULTI-STEP: No.
Rejection gate: Rejected Integration because behavior text requires real remove/recreate lifecycle "while the daemon is running".
behaviors.justification: Rebinding to a recreated directory is an OS-level watcher lifecycle problem, not just module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon, delete watched dir, recreate it, then place a new file and assert processing resumes with no daemon restart.

behaviors.description:
Observed in this behavior: simultaneous arrivals must be processed in creation-timestamp order.
Hypothetical elsewhere: business-priority ordering, cross-host clock skew.
MOCK-MISS: Partly; ordering logic can be unit/integration-tested, but real timestamp metadata and concurrent arrival races can change behavior.
REAL-INFRA: Likely yes for real failure detection under same-time arrivals and filesystem timestamp semantics.
MOCK-HIDE: Often yes, synthetic timestamps/events may not reproduce metadata resolution/race issues.
LLM-DEP: No.
MULTI-STEP: No.
Rejection gate: Rejected lower-only Integration because behavior text ties ordering to real file arrivals and creation timestamps under concurrency.
behaviors.justification: To catch true misordering from filesystem timestamp resolution and event timing, test against real files and watcher events.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files nearly simultaneously in watched directory, capture processing order, and assert it matches each file’s actual creation timestamp ordering.

behaviors.description:
Observed in this behavior: arrival event may fire before writer is done; daemon must wait until write completes before processing.
Hypothetical elsewhere: transform handling of truncated inputs after pickup.
MOCK-MISS: Yes, partial-write timing failures emerge from real writer/watcher interaction.
REAL-INFRA: Yes, requires real filesystem and concurrent write behavior.
MOCK-HIDE: Yes, mocks usually provide complete file atomically and hide early-read bugs.
LLM-DEP: No.
MULTI-STEP: No.
Rejection gate: Rejected Integration because behavior explicitly depends on in-progress writes at event time (real OS/file timing).
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real-time file-write completion detection, which in-process stubs often cannot faithfully reproduce.
behaviors.test_description: Write a large file in chunks while daemon is running, trigger arrival early, and assert processing starts only after final chunk/close and uses full file contents.
